content.problemStatement:
Chefland is holding its first ever elections!

Chefland is home to NNN people (numbered 111 to NNN), and there are MMM candidates (numbered 111 to MMM) standing for the elections.

The election in Chefland follows a ranked-choice system.


First, each person must provide their order of preference of candidates, represented by a permutation†^\dagger† of the integers 111 to MMM, say P=[P1,P2,…,PM]P = [P_1, P_2, \ldots, P_M]P=[P1​,P2​,…,PM​]
Then, candidate P1P_1P1​ will receive M−1M-1M−1 votes, candidate P2P_2P2​ will receive M−2M-2M−2 votes, and so on till candidate PMP_MPM​ receives 000 votes.

In general, candidate PiP_iPi​ will receive M−iM-iM−i votes.
This is repeated for each of the NNN people, and the total number of votes received by each candidate is tallied up.
The candidates are then placed into an order under the following rules:


If candidate iii receives more total votes than candidate jjj, then iii is placed before jjj in the order.
If candidates iii and jjj receive the same number of votes, iii is placed before jjj in the order if and only if i<ji \lt ji<j.
It can be proved that this method determines a unique total ordering of the candidates.

You are given the preferences of all NNN people of Chefland, in the form of NNN permutations P(1),P(2),…,P(N)P^{(1)}, P^{(2)}, \ldots, P^{(N)}P(1),P(2),…,P(N), each of length MMM, representing the ranked-choice orders of the NNN people.

Let AAA be the final ordering of candidates under these ranked choices. Note that AAA is also a permutation of [1…M][1\ldots M][1…M].

You're curious as to whether the elections are unstable or not.

The elections are unstable if you can remove **at most two** candidates from the election, and change the final order of the remaining candidates after recomputing the total votes - that is, the order of candidates after recomputation must **not** be a subsequence of the initial order.

Find whether the elections are unstable, and if they are, also find any set of at most two candidates whose removal causes the unstability.

†^\dagger† A permutation of the integers 111 to NNN is an array that contains each integer from 111 to NNN exactly once each.


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of multiple lines of input.

The first line of each test case contains two space-separated integers NNN and MMM — the number of people and number of candidates, respectively.
The next NNN lines describe the people's ranked choices.

The iii-th of these NNN lines contains MMM space-separated integers P1(i),P2(i),…,PM(i)P^{(i)}_1, P^{(i)}_2, \ldots, P^{(i)}_MP1(i)​,P2(i)​,…,PM(i)​ — the preference order of the iii-th person.




Output Format
For each test case:



If the election is stable, print a single line containing the integer −1-1−1.
Otherwise, print two lines.

The first line should contain an integer kkk (1≤k≤21 \leq k \leq 21≤k≤2) denoting the number of candidates you're going to remove.
The second line should contain kkk distinct integers, denoting the indices of the removed candidates.



If there are multiple possible solutions, you may print any of them.
constraints: Each P(i)P^{(i)}P(i) is a permutation of [1,2,…,M][1, 2, \ldots, M][1,2,…,M].
The sum of NNN and the sum of MMM over all test cases both won't exceed 500500500.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test case 111: There's only one voter, so no matter who is removed, the order of candidates won't change.
Test case 222: Initially, the votes of the candidates are as follows:

Candidate 111 receives 000 from the first person and 111 from the second, for 0+1=10+1 = 10+1=1 total.
Candidate 222 receives 333 from the first person and 000 from the second, for 3+0=33+0 = 33+0=3 total.
Candidate 333 receives 222 from the first person and 222 from the second, for 2+2=42+2 = 42+2=4 total.
Candidate 444 receives 111 from the first person and 333 from the second, for 1+3=41+3 = 41+3=4 total.

So, the initial order of candidates is [3,4,2,1][3, 4, 2, 1][3,4,2,1]. 333 is before 444 because they have the same number of votes, and 333 has a lower index.
After removing the candidates 333 and 444, the new preference orders are P(1)=[2,1]P^{(1)} = [2, 1]P(1)=[2,1] and P(2)=[1,2]P^{(2)} = [1, 2]P(2)=[1,2].
Now, candidate 111 and 222 both receive a total of one vote, so their order is [1,2][1, 2][1,2], with candidate 111 placing earlier by virtue of having a lower index.
[1,2][1, 2][1,2] is not a subsequence of [3,4,2,1][3, 4, 2, 1][3,4,2,1], so this election is unstable.
